mnemonics.title: RSA Security Factors
mnemonics.content: Remember: RSA relies on FACT (Factorization is Hard). The security comes from: A (Asymmetric), L (Large primes), P (Product n is public), but factoring n is computationally infeasible.

mnemonics.title: Side Channel Attacks Types
mnemonics.content: Remember 'TPE' for side channels: T - Timing, P - Power analysis, E - Electromagnetic radiation. These exploit implementation flaws, not mathematical weaknesses.

mnemonics.title: Key Exchange Attacks
mnemonics.content: For Diffie-Hellman attacks: 'MITM' - Man-In-The-Middle. The attacker sits between two parties and establishes separate keys with each. Always authenticate to prevent this!

mnemonics.title: CCA Attack Levels
mnemonics.content: CCA1: Non-adaptive (can't change strategy), CCA2: Adaptive (can modify attacks based on results). Think: '1' comes before '2', so CCA1 is simpler/weaker.

acronyms.usage: Attack model where attacker can decrypt chosen ciphertexts to learn information about the encryption key or plaintext
Attack where adversary intercepts communication between two parties, establishing separate key exchanges with each
Mathematical technique used to speed up RSA decryption; also a target for fault injection attacks
RSA padding scheme that provides semantic security against chosen ciphertext attacks
Mathematical problem underlying Diffie-Hellman key exchange security
Mathematical problem providing security for elliptic curve cryptography
Attack model where attacker can encrypt plaintexts of their choosing

visualTips.visualization: Picture a lock with two keys: public key (n, e) is the open lock anyone can use to lock (encrypt), private key (d) is the only key that can unlock (decrypt). Breaking it means finding how to open the lock without the private key.

Imagine Alice and Bob wanting to share a secret color. Alice sends color to Bob, but Eve intercepts and substitutes her color. Both Alice and Bob think they share a secret with each other, but both share secrets with Eve.

Think of a password system where each correct character takes slightly longer to verify. By measuring total time, an attacker can deduce each character one by one - this is how timing attacks work on RSA.
Two locks (n1 and n2) share a key (prime p). Finding the common key (gcd) immediately breaks both locks. Visualize two bike locks with one keyhole in common - that shared key opens both.

analogies.analogy: Like mixing two colors (p and q) to make a new color (n). It's easy to mix, but extremely hard to unmix - you can't tell what original colors were used. RSA security is exactly this.

Like trying to crack a safe by listening to the clicks of the dial (timing), feeling the vibrations (power), or seeing the wear patterns on keys, rather than solving the mathematical lock combination.

Like a mailbox: anyone can drop letters (encrypt with public key), but only the mail carrier with the private key (the only key) can retrieve them (decrypt). Breaking it means finding a way to extract letters without the key.

CPA is like having a dictionary attack capability - you can encrypt any plaintext. CCA is like having a decryption oracle - you can ask 'what happens if I decrypt this?' for any ciphertext except the target.
